text: Dr. Safa explores the abdominal cavity, observing numerous adhesions between the stomach, pancreas, and retroperitoneum due to the patient's previous sleeve gastrectomy.

Dr. Safa uses a 5mm ligature to meticulously dissect adhesions, freeing the upper part of the stomach, including the fundus. He then begins dissecting the entron, which is adherent to the transverse mesocolon.
Dr. Safa begins the dissection of the duodenum, focusing on the posterior part, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon continues the dissection until reaching the pancreaticoduodenal pedicle, then begins the retroduodenal passage using a Maryland Grasper. The procedure is performed without bleeding and without impacting the pancreas.

Dr. Safa performs the sleeve gastrectomy using green endo-GIA staplers, with the previously enlarged sleeve being calibrated using a 40 French bougie. The procedure aims to avoid tightening the entrail to minimize the risk of vomiting due to the malabsorption created by the duodenal switch.
The surgeon uses a 60mm blue cartridge on the GIA stapler to section the duodenum, precisely cutting two centimeters distal to the pylorus while carefully avoiding injury to the adjacent pancreas.

The surgical team locates the ileocecal junction and measures 2.5 meters along the ileum using graspers. They begin the duodenal ileal anastomosis at this site, securing the ileum to the duodenum with barbed sutures.
The surgeon completes the second posterior layer of the duodenal ileal anastomosis and proceeds to close the anterior layer, ensuring secure attachment between the ileum and duodenum.
